eli3: Imagine two groups of students — morning learners and night learners — each described by their test score and hours slept. LDA asks: assuming both groups follow bell-curve distributions with the same spread but different centres, where should we draw the line between them?

The algorithm estimates the centre (mean) and spread (covariance) of each group from training data. For a new student, it calculates which group they're more likely to belong to, taking into account how common each group is overall.

Because it assumes both groups share the same covariance shape, the boundary ends up being a straight line (or hyperplane in higher dimensions). This makes LDA very efficient and interpretable, and it doubles as a dimensionality reduction tool.

whenWhy.title: Interpretable linear classifier with probabilistic output
whenWhy.body: LDA provides a principled probabilistic classifier when the Gaussian assumptions are reasonable. Coefficients have direct geometric interpretation as directions that separate class means while controlling within-class spread.

whenWhy.title: Dimensionality reduction for classification
whenWhy.body: LDA projects data into at most K−1 dimensions (K = number of classes) that maximally separate the classes. This makes it both a classifier and a dimensionality reduction tool — a key advantage over PCA, which is unsupervised.

whenWhy.title: When sample size is small relative to features
whenWhy.body: LDA pools the covariance estimate across all classes, requiring fewer parameters than QDA. This makes it more stable when n is not much larger than p. Regularised LDA (add λI to Σ) handles the case p > n.

whenWhy.title: Multi-class classification
whenWhy.body: LDA naturally extends to K > 2 classes by computing K linear discriminant functions and assigning the class with the highest value. No one-vs-rest or one-vs-one binarisation is needed.

deepDiveIntro: Linear Discriminant Analysis models each class as a multivariate Gaussian with class-specific mean μₖ but a shared covariance matrix Σ. Under these assumptions, the log posterior ratio between any two classes is linear in x — producing a linear decision boundary.

The derivation proceeds from Bayes' theorem: P(y=k|x) ∝ P(x|y=k)·P(y=k). Plugging in the Gaussian likelihood and simplifying, the shared covariance cancels the quadratic terms and leaves a linear discriminant function for each class.

deepDive.heading: Generative Model Assumptions
deepDive.body: This section describes the core structure of the method and the assumptions it makes about how inputs, outputs, or latent components relate.

deepDive.heading: Linear Discriminant Function
deepDive.body: Substituting the Gaussian density into Bayes' theorem and cancelling terms that don't depend on k, the log-posterior simplifies to the linear discriminant δₖ(x). Classify x to the class k that maximises δₖ(x).

deepDive.heading: Decision Boundary
deepDive.body: This is an affine hyperplane (linear in x) — hence 'Linear' Discriminant Analysis.

deepDive.heading: LDA as Dimensionality Reduction
deepDive.body: LDA can project p-dimensional data onto at most K−1 dimensions (the Fisher discriminant subspace) that maximise between-class scatter relative to within-class scatter. This is a generalised eigenvalue problem.

deepDive.heading: Regularised LDA
deepDive.body: This section explains how the method controls flexibility, reduces overfitting, and balances fit against generalization.

takeaways: LDA assumes class-conditional Gaussians with a shared covariance Σ — the shared Σ makes the boundary linear in x. [1]
The discriminant function δₖ(x) = xᵀΣ⁻¹μₖ − ½μₖᵀΣ⁻¹μₖ + log πₖ assigns x to the class with the highest value. [1]
Parameters are estimated by: class means μ̂ₖ, pooled within-class covariance Σ̂, class priors π̂ₖ = nₖ/n. [1]
LDA reduces dimensionality to K−1 dimensions, making it both a classifier and a visualisation tool for K-class problems. [1]
When p > n, the pooled covariance is singular — apply regularised LDA (shrinkage towards identity). [1]
LDA vs QDA: LDA shares Σ across classes (linear boundary), QDA allows class-specific Σₖ (quadratic boundary). [2]

edgeCases.a: LDA fails when classes have very different covariance structures (e.g. one class is tightly clustered, another is spread out) — a linear boundary cannot capture this. Use QDA for class-specific covariances, or SVM/neural networks for more complex geometries. Also fails when features are heavily non-Gaussian (e.g. count data, binary features). [2]

edgeCases.q: What is the relationship between LDA and PCA?
edgeCases.a: Both project data to a lower-dimensional space, but with different objectives. PCA is unsupervised — it finds directions of maximum variance in the data regardless of labels. LDA is supervised — it finds directions that maximise class separation (between-class variance) relative to within-class spread. LDA is usually preferred when the goal is classification. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does LDA handle class imbalance?
edgeCases.a: The prior πₖ in the discriminant function accounts for class frequency. With balanced priors (πₖ = 1/K), all classes are treated equally. With imbalanced priors, the decision boundary shifts towards rarer classes. You can override the prior in scikit-learn with the priors parameter to compensate for imbalance. [2]

Yes. After computing δₖ(x) for each class, convert to probabilities using the softmax: P(y=k|x) = exp(δₖ(x)) / Σⱼ exp(δⱼ(x)). These are the Bayes optimal posterior probabilities under the Gaussian model assumptions. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why is LDA limited to K−1 discriminant dimensions?
edgeCases.a: The between-class scatter matrix Sᴮ is formed from K class means, so it has rank at most K−1 (the K means lie in a K−1 dimensional affine subspace). Consequently, there are at most K−1 non-trivial solutions to the generalised eigenvalue problem — and therefore at most K−1 discriminant directions. [1]

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 4
James et al. — An Introduction to Statistical Learning, Ch. 4
